metadata.summary: IaC fundamentals: declarative vs imperative, tool comparison (Terraform/Pulumi/Crossplane), GitOps integration, state management, and testing strategy for infrastructure code.

content.title: Infrastructure as Code (IaC) Fundamentals
content.summary: Infrastructure as Code applies software engineering practices to infrastructure management through declarative or imperative code definitions. This enables version control, testing, automation, and consistency across environments. IaC market projected to reach USD 6.14 billion by 2033, growing at 22%+ CAGR (2025-2033).

why.p: Infrastructure as Code applies software engineering practices — version control, testing, code review, automation — to the management of infrastructure. Without IaC, environments drift over time as engineers apply manual changes without documentation. Drift leads to subtle bugs that only reproduce in production, failed deployments because staging and prod differ, and inability to recover quickly after an incident. IaC solves this by making infrastructure definitions the source of truth: any change goes through a PR, is tested in CI, and is applied idempotently.

The declarative model (Terraform, CloudFormation) lets operators specify what infrastructure should exist rather than how to create it, while the tool handles sequencing, dependencies, and drift detection. The imperative model (Ansible, scripts) explicitly codes the execution path, offering flexibility at the cost of idempotency guarantees.

list.item: Provisioning cloud infrastructure (VPCs, clusters, databases, storage) that will change over time and must remain consistent across environments.
Team environments where multiple engineers apply infrastructure changes and concurrent access must be serialized via state locking.
Compliance and audit requirements that mandate a full change history for infrastructure modifications.
Disaster recovery scenarios where an entire environment must be reproducible from code.
Multi-environment setups (dev/staging/prod) where parity between environments is critical.
GitOps workflows where infrastructure changes are triggered by PR merges, not ad-hoc CLI commands.
Throwaway personal experiments where overhead exceeds value — use cloud console or CLI directly.
Pure configuration management on existing machines — use Ansible or Chef rather than Terraform.
Kubernetes resource management inside an existing cluster — use Helm/Kustomize rather than Terraform for K8s objects.

rule.statement: Define infrastructure declaratively whenever possible — specify the desired end-state and let the tool determine the execution path.
rule.rationale: Declarative definitions are idempotent by construction: repeated applies produce the same result. Imperative scripts require manual idempotency logic and tend to accumulate special cases.

rule.statement: Follow the immutability principle: replace infrastructure rather than modifying it in-place. Use blue-green or rolling replacement patterns.
rule.rationale: Mutable infrastructure accumulates undocumented state. Replacement ensures the new instance matches the IaC definition exactly and eliminates configuration drift.

rule.statement: Store all infrastructure definitions in version control with full commit history and PR-based review.
rule.rationale: Git provides the audit trail required for compliance, enables rollback to known-good states, and enforces peer review before any change reaches production.

rule.statement: Build modular, reusable components: decompose infrastructure into composable modules with defined inputs and outputs.
rule.rationale: Modules prevent copy-paste proliferation and allow a single, tested definition to be reused across environments with different variable values.

section.name: declarative-vs-imperative
section.p: Declarative IaC (Terraform, CloudFormation, Pulumi) defines what the final state should be. The tool computes a plan showing the delta between current state and desired state, then executes only the necessary operations. Idempotency is built-in.

Imperative IaC (Ansible, shell scripts) defines the sequence of steps to reach the desired state. Idempotency must be implemented manually with conditional logic. Imperative tools excel at configuration management on existing machines and ad-hoc orchestration tasks.
Declarative tools handle drift automatically: on the next plan/apply cycle, any out-of-band change is detected and corrected. Imperative tools require explicit drift detection logic.

section.name: tool-selection

rule.statement: For multi-cloud environments, prefer Terraform (BSL license) or OpenTofu (MPL 2.0, open-source fork). For AWS-only, CloudFormation or AWS CDK are viable. For K8s-native infrastructure, use Crossplane.
rule.rationale: Multi-cloud tools provide a single workflow and state model across providers. Cloud-native tools offer deeper integration but create vendor lock-in to a single cloud's tooling and state format.

list.item: Terraform (HCL, BSL): multi-cloud, mature ecosystem, strong state management, BSL license concerns since v1.6.
OpenTofu (HCL, MPL 2.0): open-source Terraform fork, adds state encryption, community-driven, smaller ecosystem than Terraform.
Pulumi (Python/Go/TypeScript, Apache 2.0): real programming languages with loops and functions, steeper learning curve for ops teams.
Crossplane (YAML, Apache 2.0): K8s-native, managed via kubectl, requires an existing K8s cluster as control plane.

section.p: Cloud-native tools: AWS CloudFormation (YAML/JSON, AWS-only), AWS CDK (Python/TS/Java, developer-friendly constructs), Azure Bicep (Azure-only), Google Cloud Deployment Manager (GCP-only). Configuration management: Ansible (agentless, YAML), Chef (agent-based, Ruby DSL), Puppet (agent-based, large-scale), SaltStack (event-driven).

rule.statement: Implement PR-based IaC workflow: commit triggers plan, plan output posts to PR comment, merge triggers apply. Never apply from a local workstation against production.
rule.rationale: PR-based workflow enforces review before apply, provides a complete audit trail in the PR history, and prevents divergence between what was reviewed and what was applied.

list.item: Atlantis: PR-based Terraform/OpenTofu, plan output in PR comments, apply on PR merge. Best for teams already using GitHub/GitLab PRs.
Flux: continuous reconciliation with Helm, Kustomize, Terraform. Reconciles cluster state from git on every commit.
ArgoCD: continuous reconciliation for K8s. Integrates with Helm and Kustomize. Strong UI for visualizing sync status.
Spacelift: policy-driven, supports Terraform/Pulumi/CloudFormation. Enterprise drift detection and policy enforcement.
env0: workflow-based, supports Terraform/Pulumi. Self-service infrastructure with cost visibility.

section.name: state-management

rule.statement: Always use a remote backend with state locking enabled; never use local state for team or CI environments. For AWS use S3+DynamoDB; for GCP use GCS; for Azure use Azure Blob.
rule.rationale: Local state cannot be shared, has no locking, and is lost if the engineer's machine fails. Remote backends serialize concurrent operations via locking, preventing state corruption.

rule.statement: Never commit state files to Git; always enable encryption at rest; enable bucket versioning for rollback capability; restrict state access via IAM to CI/CD roles only.
rule.rationale: State files contain plaintext passwords, connection strings, and private keys. Once committed to git, secrets cannot be fully purged. Versioning enables recovery after corruption.

section.p: Remote state best practices summary: use remote storage (S3, GCS, Azure Blob, Terraform Cloud), enable state locking (DynamoDB for AWS, built-in for GCS), enable encryption at rest and in transit, enable bucket versioning for rollback, apply least-privilege IAM policies, configure automated snapshots and cross-region replication.

section.name: project-structure
section.code: infrastructure/ ├── environments/ │ ├── dev/ │ │ ├── main.tf │ │ ├── variables.tf │ │ ├── outputs.tf │ │ ├── terraform.tfvars │ │ └── backend.tf │ ├── staging/ │ └── prod/ ├── modules/ │ ├── networking/ │ │ ├── main.tf │ │ ├── variables.tf │ │ ├── outputs.tf │ │ └── README.md │ ├── compute/ │ ├── database/ │ └── storage/ ├── policies/ │ ├── sentinel/ │ └── opa/ └── scripts/ ├── bootstrap.sh └── validate.sh

rule.statement: Separate state per environment (dev/staging/prod) and per component (networking/compute/database) using the key pattern {env}/{component}/terraform.tfstate.
rule.rationale: Single-state configurations have large blast radius — one mistake can destroy all production infrastructure. Component separation limits each apply's scope to a specific subsystem.

list.item: Static analysis (tflint, checkov): syntax checking and security best-practice enforcement without cloud API calls. Fast, runs in pre-commit and CI.
Unit tests (Terratest, pytest): test individual module functionality by provisioning real resources in an isolated test account and asserting outputs.
Integration tests (Terratest): verify cross-module interaction — e.g., that a networking module and compute module can communicate as expected.
Policy tests (OPA, Sentinel): validate compliance rules such as required tags, allowed regions, and encryption requirements before apply.
Security scanning (tfsec, Snyk): detect misconfigurations like open security groups, unencrypted storage, and missing logging.
Cost estimation (Infracost): estimate monthly cost impact of a proposed change before it is applied. Integrates with PR comments.

checklist.check: Version control repository initialized with .gitignore excluding state files, secrets, and .terraform/ directory.
Pre-commit hooks installed for formatting and linting (terraform fmt, tflint).
CI/CD pipeline configured with plan-on-PR and apply-on-merge workflow.
Remote backend configured with encryption at rest and state locking.
State infrastructure provisioned as a separate bootstrapped Terraform configuration.
Environment separation implemented (dev/staging/prod with separate state files).
Module structure defined with naming conventions documented.
No hardcoded credentials anywhere in IaC code; secrets management integrated (Vault, AWS Secrets Manager).
Least-privilege IAM policies defined for all CI/CD roles.
Audit logging enabled on state storage.
Static analysis (tflint) and policy tests (OPA/Sentinel) configured.
Module README files with input/output documentation.
Drift detection scheduled as recurring CI job.
Disaster recovery procedure documented and tested.
Required tags enforced via policy; network security policies defined.

list.item: IaC tool selection: sonnet — evaluation of multi-dimensional trade-offs across tools.
GitOps integration planning: sonnet — workflow design and pipeline architecture.
Version control strategy: haiku — standard practices with well-established patterns.

section.name: llm-prompts
section.prompt:
Design infrastructure for [APPLICATION TYPE] with the following requirements:
**Functional Requirements:** - [List functional requirements]
**Non-Functional Requirements:** - Availability: [e.g., 99.9%] - Regions: [e.g., EU, US] - Scale: [e.g., 10K requests/sec] - Compliance: [e.g., GDPR, SOC2]
**Constraints:** - Budget: [e.g., $X/month] - Team expertise: [e.g., AWS experience] - Timeline: [e.g., 3 months]
**Output Requirements:** 1. Architecture diagram (text-based) 2. Component list with cloud services 3. Terraform module structure 4. Cost estimation breakdown 5. Security considerations 6. Disaster recovery approach

Help me select the right IaC tool for:
**Project Context:** - Cloud providers: [AWS only / multi-cloud / hybrid] - Team size: [X engineers] - Team experience: [Terraform / programming languages / K8s] - Existing tooling: [current tools]
**Requirements:** - [ ] Multi-cloud support - [ ] State encryption - [ ] Policy enforcement - [ ] Cost management - [ ] GitOps integration - [ ] Programming language support
**Evaluate:** 1. Terraform vs OpenTofu 2. Pulumi (if team prefers programming) 3. Crossplane (if K8s-native) 4. Cloud-native options
Provide comparison table and recommendation with reasoning.

Create a Terraform module for [RESOURCE TYPE]:
**Requirements:** - Cloud provider: [AWS/GCP/Azure] - Resource type: [e.g., VPC, EKS cluster, RDS] - Environment support: dev/staging/prod - Security: [encryption, IAM, network isolation]
**Module should include:** 1. main.tf - resource definitions 2. variables.tf - with validation rules 3. outputs.tf - useful outputs for consuming modules 4. versions.tf - provider version constraints 5. README.md - documentation with examples
**Patterns to follow:** - Use for_each over count where appropriate - Implement dynamic blocks for flexible configuration - Add lifecycle rules for critical resources - Include default tags - Handle sensitive outputs appropriately

Set up IaC security scanning for:
**Repository:** - IaC tool: [Terraform/Pulumi/CloudFormation] - CI/CD: [GitHub Actions/GitLab CI/Jenkins] - Cloud: [AWS/GCP/Azure]
**Required checks:** - [ ] Secret detection - [ ] Misconfiguration detection - [ ] Compliance policy checks - [ ] Cost estimation
**Tools to configure:** 1. tfsec or checkov for security 2. OPA/Sentinel for policy 3. Infracost for cost 4. Pre-commit hooks
Provide complete CI/CD configuration.

Create OPA/Rego policies for:
**Policy Requirements:** 1. Required tags: [Environment, Project, Team, CostCenter] 2. Allowed instance types: [t3.*, m5.*, c5.*] 3. Encryption required: [S3, RDS, EBS] 4. Network restrictions: [no 0.0.0.0/0 ingress on SSH/RDP] 5. Region restrictions: [eu-central-1, eu-west-1]
**Output:** 1. Individual policy files 2. Test cases for each policy 3. CI/CD integration configuration 4. Documentation for developers

Configure Atlantis for Terraform GitOps:
**Environment:** - Git platform: [GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket] - Cloud: [AWS/GCP/Azure] - Terraform version: [X.X.X] - Repository structure: [monorepo/multi-repo]
**Requirements:** 1. atlantis.yaml configuration 2. Repository settings (webhooks, branch protection) 3. Server deployment (K8s/Docker/VM) 4. AWS IAM role for Atlantis 5. Security best practices 6. Team workflow documentation

Set up drift detection for infrastructure:
**Infrastructure:** - IaC tool: [Terraform/Pulumi] - Resources: [approximately X resources] - Environments: [list environments]
**Requirements:** 1. Automated drift detection schedule 2. Notification on drift (Slack/email) 3. Drift report generation 4. Auto-remediation options 5. False positive handling
**Provide:** 1. Detection script/workflow 2. CI/CD integration 3. Alert configuration 4. Remediation runbook

Design disaster recovery infrastructure:
**Primary Region:** [e.g., eu-central-1] **DR Region:** [e.g., eu-west-1]
**RPO:** [e.g., 1 hour] **RTO:** [e.g., 4 hours]
**Components:** - [List critical components]
**Requirements:** 1. DR architecture design 2. Terraform modules for DR resources 3. Replication configuration 4. Failover automation 5. Failback procedure 6. Testing runbook 7. Cost estimation for DR

example.title: Terraform provider with default tags
Remote backend with locking
Variable validation
Reusable VPC module
Module composition with depends_on
Dynamic blocks for flexible configuration
Data sources and locals
OpenTofu state encryption
Pulumi Python infrastructure
example.code:
import pulumi import pulumi_aws as aws
# Create VPC vpc = aws.ec2.Vpc("main-vpc", cidr_block="10.0.0.0/16", enable_dns_hostnames=True, enable_dns_support=True, tags={ "Name": f"{pulumi.get_project()}-vpc", "Environment": pulumi.get_stack(), } )
# Create subnets using loops availability_zones = ["eu-central-1a", "eu-central-1b", "eu-central-1c"] public_subnets = []
for i, az in enumerate(availability_zones): subnet = aws.ec2.Subnet(f"public-subnet-{i}", vpc_id=vpc.id, cidr_block=f"10.0.{i}.0/24", availability_zone=az, map_public_ip_on_launch=True, tags={ "Name": f"public-subnet-{i+1}", "Type": "public", } ) public_subnets.append(subnet)
# Export outputs pulumi.export("vpc_id", vpc.id) pulumi.export("public_subnet_ids", [s.id for s in public_subnets])

example.title: Crossplane VPC and Composite Resource Definition
example.code: apiVersion: ec2.aws.crossplane.io/v1beta1 kind: VPC metadata: name: main-vpc spec: forProvider: region: eu-central-1 cidrBlock: 10.0.0.0/16 enableDnsHostNames: true enableDnsSupport: true tags: - key: Name value: main-vpc - key: ManagedBy value: crossplane providerConfigRef: name: aws-provider --- apiVersion: apiextensions.crossplane.io/v1 kind: CompositeResourceDefinition metadata: name: xnetworks.infrastructure.example.com spec: group: infrastructure.example.com names: kind: XNetwork plural: xnetworks versions: - name: v1alpha1 served: true referenceable: true schema: openAPIV3Schema: type: object properties: spec: type: object properties: region: type: string cidrBlock: type: string availabilityZones: type: array items: type: string

example.title: GitHub Actions Terraform workflow
example.code:
name: Terraform
on: push: branches: [main] paths: ['infrastructure/**'] pull_request: branches: [main] paths: ['infrastructure/**']
env: TF_VERSION: '1.6.0' AWS_REGION: 'eu-central-1'
jobs: terraform: runs-on: ubuntu-latest permissions: contents: read pull-requests: write id-token: write
steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4
- name: Configure AWS credentials uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4 with: role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }} aws-region: ${{ env.AWS_REGION }}
- name: Setup Terraform uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3 with: terraform_version: ${{ env.TF_VERSION }}
- name: Terraform Init working-directory: infrastructure/environments/prod run: terraform init
- name: Terraform Format run: terraform fmt -check -recursive
- name: Terraform Validate working-directory: infrastructure/environments/prod run: terraform validate
- name: tfsec Security Scan uses: aquasecurity/tfsec-action@v1.0.3 with: working_directory: infrastructure
- name: Terraform Plan id: plan working-directory: infrastructure/environments/prod run: | terraform plan -no-color -out=tfplan 2>&1 | tee plan.txt echo "plan<<EOF" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT cat plan.txt >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT echo "EOF" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
- name: Comment PR if: github.event_name == 'pull_request' uses: actions/github-script@v7 with: script: | const plan = `${{ steps.plan.outputs.plan }}`; github.rest.issues.createComment({ issue_number: context.issue.number, owner: context.repo.owner, repo: context.repo.repo, body: `## Terraform Plan\n\`\`\`\n${plan}\n\`\`\`` });
- name: Terraform Apply if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push' working-directory: infrastructure/environments/prod run: terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan

example.title: Atlantis configuration
example.code:
# atlantis.yaml version: 3 projects: - name: prod dir: infrastructure/environments/prod workspace: default terraform_version: v1.6.0 autoplan: when_modified: ["*.tf", "../modules/**/*.tf"] enabled: true apply_requirements: [approved, mergeable] workflow: custom
workflows: custom: plan: steps: - init - run: tflint - run: tfsec . --soft-fail - plan apply: steps: - apply

example.title: OPA policy for required tags
example.code:
# policies/required-tags.rego package terraform.policies.required_tags
import rego.v1
required_tags := ["Environment", "Project", "ManagedBy", "Team"]
deny contains msg if { resource := input.resource_changes[_] resource.change.actions[_] == "create" tags := resource.change.after.tags
missing := {tag | tag := required_tags[_]; not tags[tag]} count(missing) > 0
msg := sprintf( "Resource %s is missing required tags: %v", [resource.address, missing] ) }
deny contains msg if { resource := input.resource_changes[_] resource.change.actions[_] == "create" not resource.change.after.tags
msg := sprintf( "Resource %s has no tags defined", [resource.address] ) }

section.name: templates
section.code: infrastructure/ ├── .gitignore ├── .pre-commit-config.yaml ├── .tflint.hcl ├── README.md ├── environments/ │ ├── dev/ │ │ ├── main.tf │ │ ├── variables.tf │ │ ├── outputs.tf │ │ ├── terraform.tfvars │ │ └── backend.tf │ ├── staging/ │ └── prod/ ├── modules/ │ └── [module-name]/ │ ├── main.tf │ ├── variables.tf │ ├── outputs.tf │ └── README.md └── policies/ └── [policy-name].rego

example.title: .gitignore for Terraform
example.code:
# Terraform *.tfstate *.tfstate.* .terraform/ .terraform.lock.hcl *.tfplan crash.log override.tf override.tf.json *_override.tf *_override.tf.json
# Secrets *.pem *.key .env .env.* secrets.tfvars *-secrets.tfvars
# IDE .idea/ .vscode/ *.swp *.swo
# OS .DS_Store Thumbs.db
# Test artifacts terraform-test-*/

example.title: Pre-commit configuration for Terraform
example.code: # .pre-commit-config.yaml repos: - repo: https://github.com/antonbabenko/pre-commit-terraform rev: v1.86.0 hooks: - id: terraform_fmt - id: terraform_validate - id: terraform_docs args: - --hook-config=--path-to-file=README.md - --hook-config=--add-to-existing-file=true - --hook-config=--create-file-if-not-exist=true - id: terraform_tflint args: - --args=--config=__GIT_WORKING_DIR__/.tflint.hcl - id: terraform_tfsec

example.title: TFLint configuration
Backend configuration template
Variables template
Outputs template
tfvars template

section.code: modules/[name]/ ├── main.tf ├── variables.tf ├── outputs.tf ├── versions.tf ├── README.md └── examples/ └── basic/ ├── main.tf └── outputs.tf

example.title: Module main.tf template
Module versions.tf template
GitHub Actions full workflow template
example.code:
# .github/workflows/terraform.yml name: Terraform
on: push: branches: [main] paths: ['infrastructure/**'] pull_request: branches: [main] paths: ['infrastructure/**'] workflow_dispatch: inputs: environment: description: 'Environment to deploy' required: true default: 'dev' type: choice options: - dev - staging - prod
env: TF_VERSION: '1.6.0' AWS_REGION: 'eu-central-1'
permissions: contents: read pull-requests: write id-token: write
jobs: validate: name: Validate runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4
- name: Setup Terraform uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3 with: terraform_version: ${{ env.TF_VERSION }}
- name: Terraform Format Check run: terraform fmt -check -recursive
- name: Setup TFLint uses: terraform-linters/setup-tflint@v4
- name: Run TFLint run: tflint --recursive
- name: Run tfsec uses: aquasecurity/tfsec-action@v1.0.3 with: working_directory: infrastructure
plan: name: Plan needs: validate runs-on: ubuntu-latest strategy: matrix: environment: [dev, staging, prod] steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4
- name: Configure AWS credentials uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4 with: role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }} aws-region: ${{ env.AWS_REGION }}
- name: Setup Terraform uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3 with: terraform_version: ${{ env.TF_VERSION }}
- name: Terraform Init working-directory: infrastructure/environments/${{ matrix.environment }} run: terraform init
- name: Terraform Plan id: plan working-directory: infrastructure/environments/${{ matrix.environment }} run: terraform plan -no-color -out=tfplan
- name: Upload Plan uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4 with: name: tfplan-${{ matrix.environment }} path: infrastructure/environments/${{ matrix.environment }}/tfplan
apply: name: Apply needs: plan if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' && github.event_name == 'push' runs-on: ubuntu-latest environment: production steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4
- name: Configure AWS credentials uses: aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials@v4 with: role-to-assume: ${{ secrets.AWS_ROLE_ARN }} aws-region: ${{ env.AWS_REGION }}
- name: Setup Terraform uses: hashicorp/setup-terraform@v3 with: terraform_version: ${{ env.TF_VERSION }}
- name: Download Plan uses: actions/download-artifact@v4 with: name: tfplan-prod path: infrastructure/environments/prod
- name: Terraform Init working-directory: infrastructure/environments/prod run: terraform init
- name: Terraform Apply working-directory: infrastructure/environments/prod run: terraform apply -auto-approve tfplan

example.title: Makefile template
example.code:
# Makefile .PHONY: init plan apply destroy fmt validate lint security test
ENV ?= dev TF_DIR = infrastructure/environments/$(ENV)
init: cd $(TF_DIR) && terraform init
plan: cd $(TF_DIR) && terraform plan -out=tfplan
apply: cd $(TF_DIR) && terraform apply tfplan
destroy: cd $(TF_DIR) && terraform destroy
fmt: terraform fmt -recursive
validate: cd $(TF_DIR) && terraform validate
lint: tflint --recursive
security: tfsec infrastructure/
test: cd tests && go test -v -timeout 30m
clean: find . -name "*.tfplan" -delete find . -name ".terraform" -type d -exec rm -rf {} +
